fuzzy_prd:
We need a small scripting language interpreter that developers can embed in tools without pulling in a large external runtime. The language should feel familiar: integers, floats, booleans, strings, arrays, and objects are all first-class values; the usual arithmetic, comparison, and logical operators work as expected; variables are declared with a keyword and can be reassigned; there's a standard for-loop with an early-exit construct and a skip-to-next construct; functions are first-class and support recursion; objects have named fields and a method-call syntax that passes the object as the first argument; and there's a try/catch mechanism for structured error handling. The only built-in output operation is a print call that writes a value followed by a newline. Float literals are written with a trailing marker to distinguish them from integers. A float that has no fractional part should render without a decimal point. Bitwise operators work on integers using the same two's-complement semantics as the type-inspection layer does for fixed-width integers. When source text cannot be parsed, the run should stop immediately with a normalized error before any code executes. An assertion built-in should fail loudly with a normalized error when its condition is false. Uncaught exceptions at the top level should produce a two-line normalized report with the category and the rendered thrown value, never leaking the host runtime's exception type.

A couple quick clarifications from the questions that came in: float literals should be written with digits, a decimal point, and a trailing 'f' marker, like '3.5f' and '10.0f'. Without the 'f' a number is an integer. And just to be explicit on rendering, a float with no fractional part still prints without a decimal point, so 2.0f prints as '2'.

Also, the typeof operator should return the normalized names we've been using: 'int32' for 32-bit integers, 'string' for text, 'bool' for booleans, and 'float32' for floating-point numbers.

On methods, the call style is the colon form, 'obj:method(args)'. That passes the receiver object as the implicit first parameter. So if somebody stores a function in a field with 'obj.method = function(self) {...}' and then calls 'obj:method()', that should pass obj as 'self'.

trigger_keywords: what are the normalized error lines
thrown value rendering in error report
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Uncaught exception report is exactly two lines: 'error=uncaught_exception\nvalue=<rendered_value>'. The value rendering is the same as what print() would produce. A parse error emits exactly 'error=parse_error' (one line). An assertion failure emits 'error=assertion_failed'.

Operator precedence: * and % bind tighter than + and -. Binary +/- are left-associative. Example: '1 + 2 * 3' = 7, '1 * 2 + 3' = 5. Bitwise ops: &, |, ^, ~, <<, >> work on 32-bit signed integers with two's-complement.

trigger_keywords: how does += work on object fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The += operator performs in-place update on variables and object fields. 'obj.field += 1' is equivalent to 'obj.field = obj.field + 1'. This is used for accumulation patterns in methods.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same two's-complement semantics as the type-inspection layer does for fixed-width integers
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1.2 bitwise operators — all bitwise and shift operators (& | ^ ~ << >>) work on 32-bit signed integers with two's-complement representation, matching standard C-like behavior

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the start.md specification and the golden reference implementation for details.

feature10_objects.json.description: Create objects with named fields, read and update fields, attach a function to a field, and invoke it as a method so the receiver object is passed as the implicit first argument. Mutations performed through the method are observable on subsequent field reads.

feature11_exceptions.json.description: Raise an exception and recover from it with a protected block and a handler that binds the thrown value. The thrown value propagates outward across nested function calls, unwinding the call stack until a handler catches it; execution then continues after the protected block.

feature12_uncaught.json.description: When an exception escapes every handler and reaches the top level, the run terminates with a normalized error report carrying the rendered thrown value, rather than leaking any host runtime detail.

feature13_parse_error.json.description: When the supplied source text is not a syntactically valid program, the run terminates with a normalized parse-error report instead of executing anything.

feature1_arithmetic.json.description: Evaluate integer arithmetic expressions honoring operator precedence and unary minus, printing each computed value. Multiplication and the remainder operator bind tighter than addition and subtraction; subtraction is left-associative. Each printed integer is followed by a newline.

feature2_bitwise.json.description: Evaluate bitwise and bit-shift operators on 32-bit signed integers: bitwise AND, OR, XOR, left shift, arithmetic right shift, and bitwise complement. Each result is printed on its own line.

feature3_logic.json.description: Evaluate comparison and boolean-logic operators and print the resulting boolean. Supports ordering comparisons, short-circuit conjunction and disjunction, and logical negation. A boolean value renders as its lowercase word.

feature4_floats.json.description: Evaluate floating-point arithmetic and print the result. Floating-point literals are written with a trailing marker. A floating-point value that has no fractional part renders without a decimal point; otherwise the fractional digits are shown.

feature5_typeof.json.description: Inspect the runtime type of a value with the type-query operator and print the type name. Distinct names are produced for 32-bit integers, text strings, booleans, and floating-point numbers.

feature6_strings.json.description: Operate on immutable text strings: query the character count, index a single character by zero-based position, and concatenate two strings. Indexing yields a one-character string; concatenation joins operands left to right.

feature7_arrays.json.description: Work with ordered, mutable, zero-indexed arrays: read an element by index, query the element count, assign to an element, and iterate with a counting loop while accumulating a running total. Reading or printing elements reflects the current contents.

feature8_control_flow.json.description: Drive a counting loop with early-exit and skip control: one construct stops the loop immediately, the other abandons the current iteration and proceeds to the next. Branch selection chooses which path runs each iteration; values are printed as the loop progresses and a final total is printed afterwards.

feature9_functions.json.description: Define first-class functions, call them with arguments, and use recursion. A function may call itself; control returns the computed value to the caller. The printed result is the value produced by invoking the function.

input.program:
var add = function (a, b) { return a + b; };
print(add(3, 4));
